1: Oracle-reception formula opening the Edom oracle. Chapter 35 is structurally paired with chapter 36 (the oracle about the mountains of Israel): chapter 35 addresses Mount Seir/Edom and its fate; chapter 36 addresses Israel's mountains and their restoration. The pair forms a chiasm with 6:1-7 (oracle against Israel's mountains for apostasy) and 35 (oracle against Edom for opposing restoration).
2: sim panecha el-har Se'ir ('set your face against Mount Seir'): the prophetic direction-formula. Har Se'ir (Mount Seir) is the Edomite heartland — the mountainous territory south and east of the Dead Sea inhabited by Esau's descendants. The directional formula matches the same formula used for the oracles against Israel's mountains (6:2) and foreign nations. Edom is treated as a full theological subject, not merely a historical enemy.
3: hinneni elecha har Se'ir ('I am against you, Mount Seir'): the confrontation formula. Unatiti yadecha shedu umshamah ('I will make you a desolation and a waste'): the same terms used for Jerusalem's judgment (5:14-15; 6:14). Edom receives the same category of judgment as Israel — the equality of divine justice before covenant and non-covenant nation is maintained. The terms of desolation applied to Edom will be reversed for Israel in chapter 36.
4: arecha choreiv eten ('your cities I will make ruins'): the cities-to-ruins formula applied to Edom. Ve'atah tihyeh shemamah ('and you yourself will be a desolation'): the judgment is comprehensive — land and people. Veyada'ta ki-ani YHWH: the recognition formula applied to Edom — even a foreign nation will come to know YHWH's identity through the enacted judgment on them. The refrain-formula is not reserved for Israel alone.
5: ya'an heyot lecha eivat olam ('because you have had an ancient/eternal enmity'): eivat olam (ancient/perpetual enmity/hatred) — Edom's hostility toward Israel is not merely political but goes back to the Esau-Jacob conflict (Gen 27-28; Num 20:14-21; Obad 10-14). Vatagger et-bnei Yisrael al-yedei charev ('you handed over the Israelites to the sword'): the crime of treacherous complicity with Babylon during Jerusalem's fall — Edom aided the enemy rather than helping their kin. Obadiah 10-14 details the same accusation.
6: lachen chai-ani ('therefore, as I live'): the divine oath sealing the judgment. Vedam ered'ka ('I will make blood pursue you'): dam (blood) as the substance of the divine judgment — blood-guilt pursued by its own reckoning. Im-lo dam sane'ta udam yirdefecha ('if you did not hate blood, blood will pursue you'): a play on Edom's double relationship with blood — if they hate blood (guilt), blood (judgment) still pursues. The inescapability of bloodguilt is the main point.
7: venatati et-har Se'ir leshimamah ushemamah ('I will make Mount Seir a desolation and waste'): the doubled desolation again (cf. v.3 and 33:28). Vehikhrati mimmenu over vashav ('and I will cut off from it both the one going and the one coming back'): the elimination of traffic — human movement ceasing entirely. A land where no one walks is a land utterly emptied of life. The thoroughgoing nature of Edom's judgment mirrors the totality of Edom's enmity.
8: umaleti et-harav chelalaiv ('I will fill his mountains with his slain'): the mountains of Seir, which were Edom's geographic identity, become the mass grave of the Edomites. Begeva'otecha uvaga'yotecha uvechol-afiqecha ('on your hills, in your valleys, and in all your ravines'): the complete geography of Mount Seir saturated with the fallen. The same specificity of 'hills and mountains and valleys' used for Israel's scattering (6:3) is applied to Edom's devastation.
9: shemamot olam ettencha ('eternal desolations I will make you'): the eschatological dimension of Edom's judgment — not merely temporary destruction but permanent emptiness. Ve'arecha lo tushavnah ('and your cities will not be reinhabited'): the curse of permanent uninhabitedness. The contrast with Israel's restoration (the old ruins will be rebuilt — 36:10, 33) is deliberate: what Edom receives permanently, Israel receives temporarily and has reversed.
10: ya'an amrecha shetei hagoyim ushetei ha'aratsot li tihyena ('because you said: "The two nations and the two lands will be mine"') — the specific claim that Edom made during Jerusalem's fall: with both Israel and Judah defeated, the entire land could be claimed by Edom. Uvey YHWH sham hayah ('but YHWH was there'): the stunning counter — despite the desolation, YHWH's presence in the land persists. The land is not vacant of divine ownership even when it appears abandoned.
11: lachen chai-ani ('therefore, as I live'): second divine oath. Ve'asiti ke'apcha ukeqin'atecha asher asita missin'atecha lahem ('I will act according to your anger and according to your jealousy which you showed in your hatred of them'): the lex talionis principle at the national level — Edom's anger, jealousy, and hatred toward Israel are returned to Edom by YHWH in kind. Venoda'ti bahem ka'asher eshpotem ('and I will make myself known among them when I judge you'): the recognition-formula applied to Israel as witness of Edom's judgment.
12: veyada'ta ki-ani YHWH shama'ti et-kol-niv'otecha ('and you will know that I, YHWH, have heard all your insults'): YHWH as the one who overhears everything spoken against Israel — asher dibbarta al-harei Yisrael ('which you spoke against the mountains of Israel'). The mountains of Israel are again personified — the same mountains that in chapter 6 receive the divine oracle of judgment now receive divine defense against Edom's insults. Lemor shamemu lanu nitenu le'oclah ('saying, "They are desolate, they are given to us to devour"').
13: vatag'du alai befichem ('you spoke against me with your mouths and multiplied your words against me'): the Edomite boasting over Israel's fall is directly identified as speech against YHWH himself. The equation is significant: to insult YHWH's people in their humiliation is to insult YHWH. The covenant solidarity between YHWH and Israel means that their enemies' contempt of Israel's defeated state is contempt of YHWH's faithfulness.
14: koh amar Adonai YHWH ('thus says the Lord YHWH'): the oracle formula resets the pronouncement. Besamoach kol-ha'aretz shemamah e'eseh lach ('when all the earth rejoices I will make you desolate'): the timing of Edom's desolation — when the earth-wide restoration comes (for Israel), Edom is excluded. The joy of universal restoration is the backdrop against which Edom's permanent desolation is seen.
15: ka'asher samachta al-nachalat beit Yisrael ('as you rejoiced over the inheritance of the house of Israel because it was desolate, so I will deal with you'): the final indictment — rejoicing over Israel's humiliation. The principle of lex talionis closes the chapter: what Edom did to Israel in Edom's hour of perceived superiority, YHWH does to Edom in YHWH's hour of restoration. Shemat tihyeh har Se'ir vekhol-Edom kullah ('desolate you will be, O Mount Seir, and all Edom, all of it'): the comprehensive desolation as the final word. Veyad'u ki-ani YHWH: the refrain, now sealing Edom's recognition of the God they mocked in Israel's defeat.
